posts.title: Accounting Basics in Estonia
posts.description: Overview of key accounting regulations in Estonia for 2026: annual report thresholds, sustainability reporting deadlines, e-invoicing requirements, and upcoming changes to the Estonian Financial Reporting Standard (EFRS).
posts.content:
Important: This article provides a general overview of the Accounting Act and its amendments. It does not constitute legal or professional advice. Each business situation is unique, and we recommend consulting with our specialists to ensure your accounting practices are fully compliant with the latest regulations.

The Estonian Accounting Act has undergone several significant amendments in recent years to align with European Union directives and meet the needs of the modern business environment. As of 2026, several updates concerning report formats, digital invoicing, and sustainability reporting have come into effect. This article provides an overview of the fundamental principles of Estonian accounting, based on the Accounting Act (Raamatupidamise seadus – RPS) and its latest version (adopted 20.11.2002, last amendment effective 17.01.2025).

1. Accounting Entity and General Principles

An accounting entity is any legal or natural person operating in Estonia who must document their economic activities. According to § 2 of the Act, this includes all private and public legal entities registered in Estonia, sole proprietors, and branches of foreign companies.

The basic requirements for organizing accounting (§ 4) are clear:

  • All business transactions must be documented and recorded.
  • Accounting is generally kept on an accrual basis (§ 5), meaning transactions are recorded when they occur, regardless of cash flow (an exception is made for sole proprietors, who may use cash-based accounting).
  • The accounting must ensure the provision of up-to-date, relevant, objective, and comparable information.

2. Annual Report in 2026

The annual report reflects a company's performance. Its standard length is 12 months, but it can be shorter or longer (max 18 months) in the first or last year of operation.

Composition of the Report

Generally, the annual report consists of the financial statements and the management report. However, there are important distinctions based on the company's size:

  • Micro-entity (total assets under €450,000, revenue under €900,000, and fewer than 10 employees): May prepare an abridged annual report, containing only a balance sheet and a profit and loss statement (the management report is optional).
  • Small entity (total assets under €7.5 million, revenue under €15 million, and fewer than 50 employees): Prepares an abridged report but must include significant notes.
  • Medium-sized and large entities: Must prepare a full report, including a cash flow statement and a statement of changes in equity.

Important! In 2026, new increased thresholds are in effect (e.g., the asset threshold for a small entity has risen to €7.5 million). This means many companies that previously qualified as small entities can now maintain this status.

3. New Regulation: Sustainability Reporting (CSR)

One of the most significant changes included in the amendment from early 2025, affecting 2026 reporting, is the regulation for the sustainability report (part of the management report).

Good news! Following an initiative by the European Commission and amendments to the Estonian Accounting Act, the deadline for submitting the sustainability report has been postponed by two years.

  • Large entities (including those with over 500 employees) were initially required to start in 2026, but the first mandatory reporting period is now 2028 (submitted in 2029).
  • SMEs that are public-interest entities will start in 2029 (submitted in 2030).

Therefore, in 2026, most Estonian companies are not yet required to prepare a sustainability report – except for those who have voluntarily started early.

4. Digital Accounting and e-Invoices

Estonia is moving rapidly towards digital accounting. A significant change concerning e-invoices came into effect on July 1, 2025:

  • Businesses can register themselves in the commercial register as recipients of e-invoices.
  • If a company registers, they can require their suppliers to submit e-invoices.
  • By default, the European e-invoicing standard EN 16931 is used, unless the parties agree otherwise.
  • This applies to both the B2G (public procurement) and B2B sectors. While e-invoicing is not mandatory in B2B, it is becoming the market standard.

This means that by 2026, every entrepreneur must be capable of at least receiving machine-readable invoices if their partner demands it.

5. Source Documents and Record Keeping

The law has clarified requirements for source documents:

  • Source documents must be machine-processable (§ 7). This means data exchange should use a standardized data description (e.g., XML format) so that information systems can process the data automatically.
  • Documents (source documents, registers, reports) must be retained for 7 years (§ 12) from the end of the financial year in which the document was recorded.

6. Internal Accounting Rules

All accounting entities, except micro-entities, must establish internal accounting rules (§ 11). This document must describe the entire accounting process – from the chart of accounts and document workflow to asset inventory and report preparation. It is a crucial document for both tax audits and ensuring continuity within the company.

7. Estonian Financial Reporting Standard (EFRS) and New Guidelines

The Estonian Financial Reporting Standard (EFRS) is based on international standards (IFRS for SMEs). In 2025, the IASB adopted the updated consolidated text of the IFRS for SMEs. Consequently, updates to the Estonian Accounting Committee Guidelines (RTJ) are expected in 2026, likely coming into effect from the beginning of 2027 (for reporting periods starting in 2027 or later).

Key planned changes to prepare for include:

  • Sale of goods with a right of return (affects e-commerce): Revenue is recognized only for the portion not expected to be returned. Returnable goods are recorded on the balance sheet as a "return asset".
  • Business combinations: Acquisition-related costs (legal, consulting fees) are no longer added to the acquisition cost but are expensed immediately.
  • Contingent consideration: Subsequent changes in contingent consideration are recognized in the profit or loss statement, rather than as an adjustment to goodwill.

Summary

In 2026, Estonian accounting regulations are stable but technologically more demanding. Although the deadline for sustainability reporting has been postponed, companies must be prepared for digitally functional accounting and the receipt of e-invoices. It is also wise to familiarize yourself with the upcoming RTJ changes now to ensure smooth reporting in 2027.

Do you need assistance organizing your company's accounting or preparing an annual report in compliance with the 2026 requirements? Feel free to contact us!

posts.title: VAT Rules for Small Businesses in Estonia in 2026
posts.description: Complete guide to VAT thresholds, special procedures for small businesses, and cross-border sales rules for Estonian entrepreneurs
posts.content:
Important: This article provides general information about VAT rules applicable in 2026. Tax laws can be complex and every business situation is unique. The thresholds and rules described here are based on the Value-Added Tax Act as of 2026, but professional advice is recommended before making decisions that could affect your tax liability.

Short answer: VAT is simpler than it looks (once you know the rules)

Many entrepreneurs panic when they first hear about VAT. But the system is actually logical: you charge VAT on your sales, reclaim VAT on your purchases, and pay the difference to the tax authority. The tricky part? Knowing when you must register and when you can stay small. Let's break it down.

2026 update: The VAT landscape has become significantly more entrepreneur-friendly thanks to the EU-wide special procedure for small businesses. Cross-border selling is now much simpler, and the thresholds have been clarified. This article reflects all changes effective as of January 1, 2026.

1. Who must register? The general obligation

The basic rule is refreshingly simple:

If your taxable supply in Estonia exceeds €40,000 in a calendar year, you must register as a VAT taxable person.

Key details:

  • Calculation period: The threshold is calculated from the beginning of the current calendar year (January 1st). No more retroactive calculations based on previous years.
  • When it triggers: The obligation arises on the exact day your turnover exceeds €40,000, not at the end of the month or quarter.
  • Exception: If your entire turnover consists of exempt supplies (like health services) or 0% rate supplies (like exports), you don't need to register — even if you exceed €40,000.

Example: An IT consultant starts in January 2026. In September, their invoices total €42,000. They must register immediately and start charging VAT from that day. Before September, all invoices were VAT-free.

2. Game-changer: Special procedure for small businesses (VAT Act § 19¹)

Since January 1, 2025, Estonian small businesses have access to a powerful EU-wide simplification. This is perhaps the most important change for small exporters.

The problem it solves:

Previously, if you sold goods to consumers in Germany, you had to either register for VAT in Germany (costly and bureaucratic) or use the One Stop Shop (OSS) system and charge German VAT from Estonia. Both added complexity.

The solution:

The special procedure for small businesses lets you apply for an "EX" suffix registration number from the Estonian Tax and Customs Board. This number tells other EU countries: "This business is exempt from registering here because it's small in its home country."

Conditions (must meet BOTH):

  1. EU-wide supply under €100,000: Your total supply across the entire European Union (including Estonia) must not exceed €100,000 in the current or previous calendar year.
  2. Destination country threshold: Your supply in that specific member state must not exceed that country's own small business threshold. These vary by country (e.g., Germany €100,000, France €85,800, etc.).

Example: An Estonian craft seller has total EU sales of €80,000, with €15,000 going to Germany. Germany's threshold is €100,000. They can apply for the EX number and sell to German consumers without registering in Germany or charging German VAT.

✨ This means you can sell across the EU with zero additional VAT bureaucracy — as long as you stay under the limits!

3. The €10,000 threshold: Digital services and distance selling

Even if you don't use the special procedure above, another important threshold applies specifically to cross-border B2C sales.

What it covers:

  • Intra-Community distance selling — goods sold to consumers in other EU countries
  • Electronically supplied services — digital products, apps, e-books, streaming, online courses
  • Telecom and broadcasting services

The rule:

  • Below €10,000/year total to all EU countries: You can keep charging Estonian VAT at Estonian rates. Simple.
  • Above €10,000/year: The destination principle kicks in. You must charge VAT at the rate of the customer's country.

But don't panic — this is where the One Stop Shop (OSS) comes in. Instead of registering in every country, you declare everything through Estonia's OSS portal once per quarter. One declaration, one payment, one place.

Important: The €10,000 threshold applies per business, not per country. Once you cross it, all your cross-border B2C digital sales become subject to destination country rates — not just the amount above the threshold.

4. The reverse charge: Taxable person with limited liability (VAT Act § 21)

As a small business, you might not be a "regular" taxable person, but you could become a taxable person with limited liability. This sounds scary, but it's actually a common situation.

When it happens:

You must register as a taxable person with limited liability if:

  • You receive services from abroad (consulting, advertising, software, legal advice) from a foreign provider not registered in Estonia, and those services are taxable here.
  • Your intra-Community acquisitions of goods exceed €10,000 in a calendar year.

What it means:

You become responsible for calculating and paying the VAT on those purchases yourself. This is called the reverse charge. You don't actually pay money to the foreign supplier — instead, you declare the VAT in your own return and simultaneously reclaim it (if you have the right to deduct). Net effect is often zero, but the paperwork must be done.

Example: A small Estonian marketing agency buys €5,000 worth of software from a US company. The US company doesn't charge VAT. The agency must register as a limited liability taxable person, declare €1,200 VAT (24%) on this purchase, and can immediately deduct it (if used for business). Net cost to the agency: still €5,000.

5. VAT rates in 2026

Here are the rates you'll actually charge (if registered):

RateApplies to
24%Standard rate — most goods and services
13%Accommodation services (hotels, hostels, short-term rentals)
9%Books, educational literature, press publications (print and digital), certain medicinal products and sanitary items
0%Exports, intra-Community supplies, international transport

Supply exempt from tax (no VAT charged, but also no input VAT deduction):

  • Health services
  • Insurance services
  • Leasing or letting of immovable property (except short-term accommodation and parking)
  • Financial services

⚠️ Critical point: If you make exempt supplies, you generally cannot deduct input VAT on related purchases. This is a major consideration for businesses like healthcare providers or landlords.

6. Accounting simplifications for small businesses

VAT isn't the only thing simplified for small businesses. The Accounting Act offers significant relief based on size.

Micro-undertaking (must meet at least two):

  • Total assets < €450,000
  • Revenue < €900,000
  • Average employees < 10

Simplification: May prepare an abridged annual report — balance sheet and profit and loss account only. No cash flow statement, no equity statement required.

Small undertaking (must meet at least two):

  • Total assets < €7.5 million
  • Revenue < €15 million
  • Average employees < 50

Simplification: Also eligible for the abridged annual report. Complex reporting is optional.

Bottom line: Unless you're a medium or large business, your statutory reporting can be refreshingly simple. Focus on running your business, not preparing complicated financial statements.

7. Practical summary: A checklist for small business owners

Before you start selling:

  • Estimate your annual turnover. Will it exceed €40,000? If yes, plan to register.
  • Check your EU sales. Will you sell to consumers in other EU countries? If yes, consider the special procedure for small businesses.
  • Review your purchases from abroad. Are you buying services from non-Estonian providers? You may need to register as a limited liability person.

If you're already selling:

  • Monitor your turnover monthly. The €40,000 threshold triggers on the day it's exceeded.
  • Track your EU sales separately. Know when you approach the €10,000 threshold for digital services/distance selling.
  • Consider your company size. Are you a micro or small undertaking? You may qualify for simplified accounting.

8. Common pitfalls to avoid

🚫 Registering too late

The obligation triggers on the day turnover exceeds €40,000. If you register a month later, you owe VAT on all invoices from that day — and may face penalties.

🚫 Ignoring the reverse charge

Buying services from abroad without registering as a limited liability person is technically tax evasion. The foreign supplier won't charge VAT, but you're supposed to declare it. Most small businesses don't realize this.

🚫 Confusing exempt with 0%

Exempt means no VAT charged AND no input VAT recovery. 0% means no VAT charged BUT you can still reclaim VAT on related costs. Different rules, big difference.

🚫 Missing the €10,000 threshold

Once you cross it, all your cross-border B2C digital sales become taxable in the customer's country — not just the excess. This catches many businesses off guard.

Summary: Your VAT decision tree for 2026

  1. Turnover in Estonia > €40,000? → Must register as taxable person.
  2. Selling to EU consumers? → Consider the special procedure for small businesses (EX number).
  3. Selling digital services to EU consumers > €10,000? → Use One Stop Shop (OSS) to charge destination country VAT.
  4. Buying services from abroad? → Check if you need to register as limited liability person and apply reverse charge.
  5. Small company? → Enjoy simplified accounting — abridged annual report only.

🎯 The bottom line: VAT in Estonia is logical and increasingly entrepreneur-friendly. The 2025/2026 changes — especially the special procedure for small businesses — have removed many of the barriers to cross-border trade. The key is knowing your thresholds, monitoring them regularly, and getting advice when you're unsure.

When in doubt, talk to an accountant. The cost of advice is tiny compared to the cost of mistakes.

Try our VAT calculator to see how much VAT you'll need to charge or reclaim. And if you're unsure about your registration status, contact us for a consultation.

Article prepared on March 13, 2026, based on the Value-Added Tax Act (RT I, 03.12.2024, 2 – effective 01.01.2025 and 01.01.2026) and the Accounting Act (RT I, 07.01.2025, 1 – effective 17.01.2025).

posts.title: Payroll Calculation in 2026
posts.description: Correct payroll calculation with all taxes including the new 2% security tax. Real examples, formulas, and important updates for 2026.
posts.content:
Important: This article is for informational purposes and describes tax calculation rules according to Estonian legislation. Each business situation is unique — we recommend consulting with our specialists to ensure your payroll processes are fully compliant.

Why Payroll Calculation Matters in 2026

Correct payroll calculation is the foundation of a successful employment relationship and a guarantee of avoiding problems with the tax authority. In 2026, several well-established taxation principles continue to apply in Estonia, but an important new element is being added — the security tax. In this article, we will explain how to correctly calculate wages in 2026, which taxes and from whom to withhold, and what benefits employees can use.

The article is based on the latest versions of the laws in force in 2026, including the new Security Tax Act that came into effect on January 1, 2026.

2026 Update: All employers must now withhold an additional 2% security tax from employee gross wages. This tax is calculated on the full gross amount before any deductions (including tax-exempt income).

1. Initial Data: Minimum Wage and Tax Rates for 2026

Before starting calculations, we need to know the basic parameters set by the state for 2026.

Minimum Wage

As of January 1, 2025 (valid for 2026 calculations):

  • Monthly minimum wage: €886 (for full-time employment)
  • Hourly minimum wage: €5.31

Source: Government Regulation No. 87 of 19.12.2024

Tax Rates and Payments on Wage

Tax/PaymentEmployee RateEmployer RateLegal Basis
Income Tax22%–Income Tax Act § 4(1)
Social Tax–33%Social Tax Act § 7(1)
Unemployment Insurance1.6%0.8%Unemployment Insurance Act § 41
Security Tax (NEW)2%–Security Tax Act § 3
Funded Pension (II pillar)2%, 4% or 6%–Funded Pensions Act § 9

Note: Unemployment insurance rates are indicative and confirmed annually by government regulation.

2. Important Innovation for 2026: The Security Tax

The Security Tax Act entered into force on January 1, 2026. This tax applies to both the income of natural persons and the profits of companies.

Key Points for Payroll:

  • Who pays: The tax is withheld from the employee's gross income.
  • Rate: 2% of the employee's gross salary.
  • Object of taxation: The tax is calculated on the full gross salary — no deductions allowed (Security Tax Act § 10(3)). This means the tax-exempt amount and funded pension contributions cannot be subtracted before calculating this tax.

Important: The security tax is calculated on the entire gross salary, even if the employee's income is below the tax-exempt threshold. For example, an employee earning €700/month pays €14 in security tax, even though they pay no income tax.

3. Payroll Calculation Formula (Gross → Net) including Security Tax

In 2026, the net salary (take-home pay) is calculated using the following formula:

Net = Gross – Income Tax – Unemployment Insurance (employee) – Security Tax – Funded Pension (employee)

Step-by-Step Calculation:

  1. Employee's Unemployment Insurance: Gross × 1.6%
  2. Funded Pension (II pillar): Gross × employee's chosen rate (2%, 4%, or 6%)
  3. Security Tax: Gross × 2%
  4. Income Tax Base: Gross – Unemployment – Funded Pension – Tax-Exempt Amount
  5. Income Tax: Income Tax Base × 22%
  6. Net Salary: Gross – All deductions

3.1. Tax-Exempt Income in 2026

An important change applies from 2026 (Income Tax Act § 23 and § 235):

  • Persons under 65 (before retirement age): Fixed tax-exempt amount of €700 per month
  • Persons of retirement age: Fixed tax-exempt amount of €776 per month

The previous complex formula with income-dependent reductions has been abolished.

3.2. Calculation Examples

Example 1: Young Specialist (30 years old, €1800 gross)

  • Gross salary: €1,800
  • Funded pension rate: 2%
  • Tax-exempt income: €700
Unemployment Insurance (1.6%):€28.80
Funded Pension (2%):€36.00
Security Tax (2%):€36.00
Income Tax Base:€1,035.20
Income Tax (22%):€227.74
Net Salary:€1,471.46

Example 2: Pensioner (68 years old, €1200 gross)

  • Gross salary: €1,200
  • Funded pension: 0% (exempt)
  • Tax-exempt income: €776
Unemployment Insurance (1.6%):€19.20
Funded Pension (0%):€0.00
Security Tax (2%):€24.00
Income Tax Base:€404.80
Income Tax (22%):€89.06
Net Salary:€1,067.74

4. Employer's Taxes and Payments (added to gross salary)

The employer must calculate and pay taxes that are added on top of the employee's gross salary. The security tax does not increase the employer's direct costs — it is only withheld from the employee and forwarded to the state.

  • Social Tax: Gross × 33%
  • Employer's Unemployment Insurance: Gross × 0.8%

4.1. Example: Total Employer Cost

Continuing with Example 1 (young specialist, €1800 gross):

Gross Salary:€1,800.00
Social Tax (33%):€594.00
Employer Unemployment (0.8%):€14.40
Total Employer Cost:€2,408.40

Note: The security tax (€36) and income tax are withheld from the employee's gross salary and do not directly increase employer costs. However, the employer is responsible for correctly calculating, withholding, and transferring these amounts to the Tax and Customs Board.

5. Special Cases and Exemptions

5.1. Employees with Reduced Work Ability

Employers can claim a refund of part of the paid social tax for employees with partial or no work ability (Social Tax Act § 6). This is an important state support measure for promoting employment.

5.2. Exemption from Funded Pension

The following employees are exempt from II pillar contributions:

  • Employees of retirement age
  • Employees with established partial or no work ability
  • Employees who have submitted an application to suspend payments

Employers must track these exemptions and not deduct funded pension from exempt employees' wages.

5.3. Security Tax Exemptions?

The Security Tax Act does not provide standard deductions (like tax-exempt income). However, the tax is not withheld from certain types of income that are not subject to income tax at all. But if the income is taxable, the security tax applies to its full amount.

6. Employer Responsibilities: Deadlines and Declarations

6.1. Payment Deadlines

All taxes and contributions must be transferred to the Tax and Customs Board's bank account by the 10th day of the month following the payment month. This includes:

  • Income tax
  • Social tax
  • Unemployment insurance premiums (both employee and employer parts)
  • Security tax (same deadline as income tax)
  • Funded pension contributions

6.2. Monthly Declaration (TSD)

By the same 10th day deadline, employers must submit the monthly TSD declaration, which now includes fields for reporting withheld security tax amounts.

6.3. What Employers Must Track

  • Employee's chosen funded pension rate (2%, 4%, or 6%) — check in e-MTA
  • Whether employee is exempt from funded pension
  • Employee's tax-exempt income status (regular vs. pensioner)
  • Correct application of the new 2% security tax to all taxable wages

7. Summary: What's New for 2026

Area20252026
Security TaxNot applicable2% on gross wages (withheld from employee)
Tax-Exempt IncomeFormula-based (max €654)Fixed: €700 (regular) / €776 (pensioners)
Income Tax Rate22%22% (unchanged)
Social Tax33%33% (unchanged)
Minimum Wage€886€886 (unchanged from 2025)

🎯 The Bottom Line for 2026: Payroll calculation becomes slightly more complex due to the introduction of the 2% security tax, but the system remains transparent. The key changes are:

  1. Withhold an additional 2% security tax from all employee gross wages
  2. Apply the new fixed tax-exempt amounts (€700/€776)
  3. Continue using the same rates for income tax (22%), social tax (33%), and unemployment insurance

With correct calculations and timely declarations, you can avoid tax arrears, penalties, and ensure smooth operations with the Tax and Customs Board.

Try our interactive salary calculator with your own figures, and contact us for a consultation — we'll help ensure your payroll processes are fully compliant and optimized for 2026!

posts.title: AML Requirements for Estonian Companies: A Complete Guide for 2026
posts.description: Comprehensive overview of Anti-Money Laundering obligations for Estonian businesses — who must comply, what procedures to implement, and how to avoid fines up to €5 million.
posts.content:
Important: This article provides general information about AML requirements in Estonia based on the Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing Prevention Act (RahaPTS). It does not constitute legal advice. Each company's obligations may vary depending on its specific activities and risk profile. We recommend consulting with qualified legal and compliance professionals for advice tailored to your situation.

AML is not just a buzzword — it's a legal obligation

Anti-Money Laundering (AML) regulations in Estonia are strict, comprehensive, and enforced with significant penalties. Ignoring these requirements can lead to fines of up to €5 million, criminal liability for directors, or even license revocation.

In this guide, we break down who is subject to the Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing Prevention Act (RahaPTS), what procedures must be implemented, and how to avoid critical mistakes in 2026.

2026 Update: Estonian AML requirements remain fully aligned with EU's 5th and 6th Anti-Money Laundering Directives. Key focus areas include beneficial ownership transparency, enhanced due diligence for high-risk countries, and strict rules for virtual asset service providers (VASPs).

1. Who is an "Obliged Entity"? (Who must comply?)

The AML law applies to a much wider range of businesses than most people think. According to § 2 of the Act, the list includes:

Financial Institutions

  • Credit institutions (banks)
  • Currency exchange offices
  • Payment and e-money institutions
  • Insurance companies and intermediaries
  • Investment firms and fund managers

Virtual Asset Service Providers (VASPs)

  • Cryptocurrency exchanges
  • Crypto wallet providers
  • ICO issuers and platforms

Professional Service Providers

  • Notaries, attorneys, bailiffs, bankruptcy trustees — when handling client funds or facilitating financial/real estate transactions
  • Auditors, accountants, tax advisors — when providing services listed in the Act
  • Trust and company service providers — formation agents, legal address providers, nominee directors

Non-Financial Sector (High-Value Dealers)

  • Cash traders: Anyone receiving cash payments of €10,000 or more (including linked transactions)
  • Real estate agents and rental intermediaries: For transactions or monthly rent ≥ €10,000
  • Precious metals/stones and art dealers: Transactions ≥ €10,000
  • Gambling operators: Casinos, online betting platforms

NGOs and Foundations

  • When receiving or making cash payments exceeding €5,000

⚠️ Warning: Even if your main business is not financial, a single cash transaction over €10,000 makes you an "obliged entity" for that transaction, with full reporting requirements.

2. The Core Principle: Know Your Customer (KYC)

At the heart of AML compliance is customer due diligence (CDD). You must know who your client is, understand their business, and verify their information.

When must you apply CDD measures? (§ 19)

  • When establishing a business relationship (opening an account, starting cooperation)
  • For occasional transactions ≥ €15,000 (or a series of linked transactions)
  • Whenever there is a suspicion of money laundering (regardless of amount)
  • When you doubt previously obtained data (e.g., client's information seems inconsistent)

What does CDD include? (§ 20)

The standard "KYC package" requires you to:

  1. Identify the client — Verify identity using a valid passport/ID card (in person or via secure remote identification as per § 31, such as Smart-ID or video identification).
  2. Verify representatives — Check their authority to act on behalf of the client.
  3. Identify the beneficial owner — Determine who ultimately owns or controls the entity (see section 3 below).
  4. Understand the business relationship — Know the purpose of the account, source of funds, and expected transaction patterns.
  5. Check PEP status — Determine if the client or their family members are Politically Exposed Persons (requires enhanced due diligence under § 41).
  6. Ongoing monitoring — Continuously review transactions to ensure they match the client's profile.

3. The Beneficial Owner: The Most Critical Detail

Requirements for identifying and registering beneficial owners are detailed in Chapter 9 of the Act. This is where many Estonian companies get into trouble.

Who is a beneficial owner? (§ 9)

A natural person who, directly or indirectly:

  • Owns more than 25% of shares or voting rights
  • Exercises control by other means (agreements, veto rights, etc.)
  • If no such person exists, senior management must be registered as "pseudo-beneficial owners"

Registration requirements (§ 77)

Data must be entered into the Beneficial Ownership Information Database (kasuliku tegeliku kasusaaja andmekogu) via the e-Business Register portal.

  • Deadline: Submit upon company registration and update within 30 days of any change
  • Verification: Notaries, banks, and other obliged entities must verify your data against the register
  • Public access: Information is accessible to authorities and obliged entities (limited public access with legitimate interest)

Penalties for non-compliance (§ 95)

  • For legal persons: Up to €400,000
  • For natural persons (directors): Fines and potential disqualification
  • Repeated violations: Can lead to forced dissolution of the company

✨ Pro tip: Check your company's data in the register today. Even if you submitted it years ago, any change in ownership structure must be updated within 30 days.

4. Internal Procedures: Your AML Shield

Identifying clients is not enough. You must have documented systems and trained staff. §§ 13 and 14 require:

Conduct a Risk Assessment

You must understand the risks inherent in your business model, client base, and geographic reach. Consider:

  • Client risk (high-risk industries, PEPs, complex structures)
  • Product/service risk (cash-intensive, cross-border, anonymous)
  • Geographic risk (countries with weaker AML regimes)

Develop Internal Rules (Protseduurireeglid)

This document must clearly outline:

  • How you identify clients and verify their identity
  • How you determine and document risk profiles
  • How you identify and report suspicious transactions
  • How you retain records (minimum 5 years as per § 51)
  • Roles and responsibilities of staff

Appoint a Responsible Person (§ 17)

High-risk obliged entities (banks, crypto firms, payment institutions) must designate a compliance officer responsible for:

  • Implementing AML procedures
  • Liaising with the Financial Intelligence Unit (FIU)
  • Receiving and acting on internal suspicious transaction reports

Train Employees (§ 14(6))

Regular AML training is mandatory for all relevant staff. Training must cover:

  • Current AML laws and regulations
  • Internal procedures and reporting channels
  • How to recognize red flags
  • Consequences of non-compliance

5. Suspicious Transactions: When to Sound the Alarm?

§ 49 is the most critical provision for any obliged entity. If you notice something unusual, you must report it to the Financial Intelligence Unit (FIU) immediately, but no later than within 2 working days.

What are "red flags"?

  • Complex, illogical transaction structures with no economic purpose
  • Client refuses to provide information about beneficial ownership
  • Transactions from accounts in high-risk countries (as listed by FATF or EU)
  • Urgent requests to transfer funds immediately after account opening
  • Transactions that are unusually large compared to normal activity
  • Client seems unconcerned about high fees or unfavorable terms
  • Use of cash when normal business practice would use banking channels

Reporting procedure

  1. Internal report: Employee reports suspicion to the designated responsible person
  2. Analysis: Responsible person evaluates if suspicion is founded
  3. External report: If confirmed, submit to FIU via their secure portal within 2 working days
  4. Tipping off prohibition: You CANNOT inform the client that a report has been made (§ 93)

Safe harbor: The law protects you if you report a suspicion in good faith, even if it turns out to be unfounded. You cannot be sued for breach of confidentiality.

6. Prohibition on Anonymity (§ 25)

The Act strictly prohibits anonymous accounts, passbooks, or crypto-wallets. Any business relationship must be personalized and traceable.

  • No numbered accounts: All accounts must be in the name of an identified person
  • No anonymous transactions: All transactions must be attributable to an identified client
  • No shell companies: Clients cannot use corporate vehicles to hide ownership without disclosure

7. Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD) — §§ 40-43

For higher-risk situations, standard CDD is not enough. You must apply enhanced measures:

When is EDD required?

  • PEPs and their family members (§ 41) — Establish source of wealth and funds
  • Cross-border correspondent relationships (§ 42) — For financial institutions
  • Complex or unusually large transactions
  • Clients from high-risk third countries (as listed by EU)
  • Whenever normal CDD raises suspicion

EDD measures include:

  • Additional information on the client's business and ownership
  • Source of wealth and source of funds documentation
  • Senior management approval to establish/continue relationship
  • Enhanced monitoring of transactions

8. Penalties for Violations (Chapter 10)

Estonian AML penalties are severe and actively enforced by the FIU and supervisory authorities.

For legal persons:

  • Up to €5 million OR up to 10% of annual global turnover (whichever is higher)
  • License suspension or revocation
  • Public naming and reputational damage
  • Forced dissolution in extreme cases

For natural persons (directors, responsible persons):

  • Large personal fines
  • Disqualification from serving as a director
  • Criminal liability for intentional violations (up to 3 years imprisonment)

Most common violations:

  • Failure to identify beneficial owner (up to €400,000 for companies)
  • Failure to report suspicious transactions
  • Inadequate internal procedures or training
  • Poor record-keeping

9. Exceptions for Legal Professionals (§ 45)

Attorneys, notaries, and auditors have specific exemptions when the information is protected by legal privilege.

When are they exempt?

  • When defending a client in court proceedings
  • When providing legal advice regarding ongoing or contemplated litigation

When are they NOT exempt?

  • When forming companies or providing trust services
  • When handling client funds
  • When acting as financial intermediaries
  • When suspicion arises outside privileged activities

10. Record-Keeping Requirements (§ 51)

All obliged entities must maintain records for at least 5 years after:

  • The end of a business relationship
  • The date of an occasional transaction

What must be kept?

  • Copies of identification documents
  • Transaction records
  • Risk assessments
  • Suspicious transaction reports (including internal analysis)
  • Training records

⚠️ After 5 years, data must be securely deleted, unless required for ongoing legal proceedings.

Summary: A Checklist for Company Directors

✅ Your AML To-Do List for 2026

  • Register beneficial owners — Check the e-Business Register today. Is your data correct and up-to-date?
  • Implement KYC procedures — Do you have a documented process for identifying new clients?
  • Draft internal rules — Create a written AML procedures document (required for all obliged entities).
  • Train your staff — Conduct AML training at least annually and keep records.
  • Know your red flags — Ensure your team can spot suspicious transactions.
  • Establish reporting lines — Know who to contact at the FIU and how.
  • Retain records — Set up a system to store AML documents for 5 years.
  • Review high-risk clients — Apply enhanced due diligence where needed.

Key Takeaways

  • AML applies to more businesses than you think — Even a single €10,000 cash transaction triggers obligations.
  • Know your beneficial owner — This is the single most common compliance failure.
  • Document everything — If it's not documented, it didn't happen.
  • Report suspicions — The FIU takes non-reporting very seriously.
  • Penalties are severe — Up to €5 million for companies.

🎯 The bottom line: AML compliance in Estonia is not optional — it's a legal requirement with real teeth. But with proper procedures, trained staff, and regular monitoring, compliance is manageable. The cost of getting it wrong (fines, reputational damage, criminal liability) far outweighs the investment in getting it right.

If you need help developing your internal AML rules, training your team, or reviewing your compliance status, contact our experts. We help Estonian companies stay on the right side of the law.

Contact us for a compliance review or to discuss your specific AML obligations.

posts.title: Freelance vs Employment in Estonia: Which Pays Off in 2026?
posts.description: Detailed tax analysis, real calculations including the security tax, and optimal strategies for freelancers and employees
posts.content:
Important: This article is for informational purposes and describes legal tax optimization methods provided by Estonian legislation. Paying minimum wage must correspond to actual work activities. Artificially lowering your salary without economic justification is unacceptable. Every situation is unique — we recommend consulting our specialists to calculate the optimal strategy for your specific case.

The short answer: it's more complicated than it seems

Many people think employment is better because "someone else pays your taxes". In reality, your employer pays taxes from their own pocket, not from your salary. In 2026, there's also a new security tax (2%) that needs to be factored in. Let's break it down with real numbers.

Note: Estonian legislation gives entrepreneurs the choice between different business forms and income strategies. For example, registering as an FIE (sole proprietor), you can pay social tax only on the minimum wage — this is explicitly allowed by law. The rest of your income is only subject to income tax and security tax. However, your actual activities must match your chosen model — our accountants can help you find the right balance between optimization and compliance.

Comparison: €3,000 monthly income

Scenario 1: Employee

  • Gross salary: €3,000
  • Taxes:
    • Income tax: €506 ((3,000-700)×22%)
    • Unemployment (1.6%): €48
    • Pension (2%): €60
    • Security tax (2%): €60
  • Net take-home: €2,326
  • Actual employer cost: €4,014 (€3,000 + €990 social tax + €24 unemployment)

Scenario 2: Freelancer (worst case - no optimization)

  • Income: €3,000
  • Taxes:
    • Income tax: €506
    • Social tax (33%): €990
    • Security tax (2%): €60
    • Pension (2%, optional): €60
  • Net take-home: €1,384 (€1,444 without pension)

⚠️ This is definitely not the way to pay taxes!

Scenario 3: Freelancer as FIE (sole proprietor - optimized)

Register as an FIE (sole proprietor) and pay social tax only on the minimum wage (in 2026 minimum wage is €886):

  • Income: €3,000
  • Social tax (on €886): €292.38
  • Income tax: €506
  • Security tax (2%): €60
  • Net take-home: €2,141.62

✨ Already close to the employee's €2,326, but with entrepreneur benefits!

Scenario 4: Freelancer via OÜ (Ltd) - optimal for higher incomes

Once your income exceeds €25,000-30,000/year, it's better to open a company:

  • Revenue: €3,000
  • Pay yourself minimum wage (€886):
    • Income tax: €41 ((886-700)×22%)
    • Unemployment: €14.18
    • Pension: €17.72
    • Security tax: €17.72
    • Net from salary: €795.38
  • Left in company: €2,114
  • Corporate income tax when withdrawing as dividends (22/78): €465
  • Net dividends: €1,649
  • Total take-home: €2,444

✨ That's €118 more than the employee!

Fair comparison: same client budget

If your client is willing to pay €4,014 (the same as an employee costs):

ParameterEmployeeFreelance (OÜ)
Client budget€4,014€4,014
Your after-tax income€2,326€2,950
Difference+€624 in favor of freelancing!

Result: With the same client budget, freelancing gives you €624 more per month — that's €7,488 per year!

Step-by-step strategy for freelancers

Step 1: Start with FIE (income up to €2,000-2,500/month)

  • Register as an FIE (simple and cheap)
  • Pay social tax only on the minimum wage (€886)
  • The rest is business income

Result: With €2,500 income, take-home ~€1,850

Step 2: Switch to OÜ (stable income from €2,000-2,500/month)

  • Open an OÜ (a bit more complex, but more profitable)
  • Pay yourself minimum wage (€886)
  • Take the rest as dividends quarterly/yearly

Result: With €3,000 income, take-home ~€2,444

Step 3: Mixed strategy (advanced level)

  • Pay yourself an optimal salary (not necessarily minimum)
  • Leave some profit in the company for investments
  • Buy assets through the company (laptop, car, tools)
  • Use business travel and representation expenses

Key 2026 nuances

  • Security tax (2%): New tax that must be included in all calculations. For employees, it's withheld from salary; for freelancers, you pay it yourself.
  • Tax-free allowance: From 2026, it's fixed at €700 for everyone (except pensioners who get €776). No more complicated formulas!
  • Sick leave: Freelancers don't get it (but you can buy insurance)
  • Vacation: You plan and save for it yourself
  • Pension: Social tax on minimum wage gives you pension qualifying years (important for your future state pension)
  • Expenses: Freelancers can deduct equipment, internet, training, and part of utilities

2026 conclusions

Freelancing is better if:

  • Your income exceeds €25,000-30,000/year
  • You're willing to handle optimization (or delegate it to an accountant)
  • You have business expenses to deduct
  • You value flexibility and can handle income instability

Employment is better if:

  • You value stability, paid sick leave, and vacation
  • You don't want to deal with paperwork and reporting
  • Your income is under €15,000-18,000/year
  • Social protection is important to you

🎯 2026 Bottom line: With an annual income of €36,000, a freelancer via OÜ takes home about €29,328, while an employee with the same cost to the employer takes home about €27,912. That's a €1,416 per year advantage for freelancing. And with expense optimization, the gap widens even further.

But the real advantage of freelancing is scalability. When your income grows to €60,000/year, the difference becomes €5,000+ per year.

Try our calculator with your numbers and book a consultation — we'll help you find the optimal strategy for your specific situation!
